text: Pazuzu, the Mesopotamian demon king of wind, was paradoxically invoked as protection against his consort Lamashtu, who threatened pregnant women and newborns. Amulets bearing his image dating to 800-600 BCE have been found across ancient Assyria.

The Ars Goetia, the first section of the Lesser Key of Solomon (compiled c. 17th century), catalogs 72 demons organized by rank: Kings, Dukes, Princes, Marquises, Counts, Knights, and Presidents, each commanding legions of spirits.

In Tibetan Buddhism, wrathful deities (Herukas) like Yamantaka appear demonic but are enlightened beings using fierce forms to destroy obstacles to awakening — a key distinction from Western demonology where demonic forms always indicate evil nature.

The Malleus Maleficarum (1487) by Heinrich Kramer became the de facto handbook for witch-hunters across Europe for nearly 200 years, legitimizing the connection between demonology and witchcraft prosecution despite being condemned by the Catholic Church's own Inquisition at Cologne.

Islamic demonology distinguishes between Jinn (created from smokeless fire), Shayatin (rebellious jinn who serve Iblis), and specific subtypes: Ifrit (powerful fire jinn), Marid (water jinn), Ghul (shape-shifting desert jinn), Si'lat (the most intelligent and dangerous), and Qareen (a personal jinn companion assigned to every human).

Ravana, the ten-headed Rakshasa king of Lanka in the Hindu epic Ramayana, was also a devout scholar of the Vedas and an accomplished veena player. His complex characterization challenges simple good-evil binaries in Hindu demonology.

The Japanese oni were originally invisible spirits of disease and calamity (oni derives from 'on' meaning hidden). Their iconic red/blue skin, horns, and iron clubs only became standardized in art during the Muromachi period (1336-1573).

Johann Weyer's De Praestigiis Daemonum (1563) was one of the first systematic arguments against witch trials, claiming that supposed witches were actually mentally ill women deceived by demons, making Weyer a pioneer in both psychiatry and skeptical demonology.

The Chinese Hungry Ghost Festival (Zhongyuan Jie) held on the 15th day of the 7th lunar month marks when the gates of hell open and hungry ghosts (egui) roam the earth. Offerings of food, joss paper, and performances are made to appease these restless spirits.

In Zoroastrianism, the Daevas are a class of demons led by Angra Mainyu (Ahriman), the cosmic adversary of Ahura Mazda. The word 'daeva' shares the same Proto-Indo-European root as Latin 'deus' (god) — what was divine in one tradition became demonic in another.

Collin de Plancy's Dictionnaire Infernal (1818, illustrated edition 1863) provided the most influential visual depictions of demons in Western art. Louis Le Breton's 69 illustrations defined how demons like Bael, Astaroth, and Belphegor are still imagined today.

The Pontianak of Malay folklore is the vengeful ghost of a woman who died during childbirth. She is said to be detectable by a sweet floral scent that turns putrid as she approaches, and can be neutralized by driving a nail into the hole at the back of her neck.

Mara, the Buddhist demon of desire and death, attempted to prevent Siddhartha Gautama's enlightenment by sending his three daughters (Tanha, Arati, and Raga — Craving, Aversion, and Passion) and armies of monsters. The Buddha's defeat of Mara by touching the earth is one of Buddhism's most depicted scenes.

The Pseudomonarchia Daemonum (1577) by Johann Weyer listed 69 demons compared to the Ars Goetia's 72, and notably omitted seals/sigils for summoning — Weyer included it as an appendix to his anti-witch-trial treatise, arguing knowledge of demons proved they were real but witchcraft was delusion.

The Asuras in Hindu mythology evolved from gods to demons over centuries. In the earliest Vedic texts, 'asura' meant 'lord' and described supreme deities including Varuna. The Asura-Deva split mirrors the Iranian reversal where 'daeva' became demonic while 'ahura' became divine.

Hieronymus Bosch's triptych The Garden of Earthly Delights (c. 1490-1510) contains the most elaborate demonic imagery in Western art history. The right panel ('Hell') depicts demons as hybrid creatures — bird-headed, knife-bodied, egg-shaped — torturing sinners with musical instruments.

The Tokoloshe of Zulu mythology is a small, hairy water sprite summoned by malevolent shamans (sangomas) to cause illness and death. Many South Africans raise their beds on bricks to avoid the Tokoloshe, who is said to be too short to reach elevated sleepers.

The possessed nuns of Loudun (1632-1634) accused Urbain Grandier, a Catholic priest, of sending demons including Asmodeus, Leviathan, Isacaaron, and Balaam into their convent. Grandier was burned alive. Modern scholars consider it a case of mass hysteria combined with political conspiracy.

In Filipino folklore, the Aswang is a shape-shifting creature that appears as an ordinary person by day but transforms at night into a form that preys on pregnant women and the sick. The Aswang has regional variants: Manananggal (self-segmenting), Tiktik (bird-like), and Wak-Wak (bat-winged).

Azazel appears in the Book of Enoch as a fallen Watcher angel who taught humans metallurgy, weaponry, and cosmetics — corrupting humanity through knowledge. In Leviticus 16, the scapegoat ritual sends sins into the wilderness 'for Azazel,' possibly referencing this same entity.

The Munich Manual of Demonic Magic (Clm 849, 15th century) is one of the few surviving medieval grimoires written by a cleric. It contains rituals for summoning demons to obtain knowledge, treasure, and the love of women, complete with elaborate circles of protection.

Goya's 'Black Paintings' (1819-1823), painted directly on the walls of his home, include 'Witches' Sabbath' depicting a massive goat-devil (representing Satan) presiding over a coven. These works are considered the most psychologically intense depictions of demonic themes in art history.

The Abiku in Yoruba tradition are spirits of children who die young and are reborn repeatedly to the same mother, causing cycles of grief. Special rituals including scarification marks on the child's body are performed to make the spirit want to stay in the living world.

Asmodeus, described in the Book of Tobit as the demon who killed Sarah's seven husbands, became associated with lust in medieval demonology. In the Dictionnaire Infernal, he has three heads (bull, man, ram), rides a dragon, and commands 72 legions.

Robert Johnson's legend of selling his soul to the devil at a Mississippi crossroads around 1930 draws from African-American hoodoo traditions where crossroads are liminal spaces between worlds, supervised by the trickster figure Papa Legba (via Haitian Vodou influence).

The Compendium Maleficarum (1608) by Francesco Maria Guazzo systematized demonic pacts into explicit and implicit categories, described eleven steps of a witches' sabbath, and provided detailed illustrations of demons that influenced European demonological imagery for centuries.

Shuten-dōji, Japan's most famous oni, led a band of demons on Mount Oe during the Heian period (794-1185). The legendary warrior Minamoto no Raikō defeated him using enchanted sake. The tale represents anxieties about banditry and social disorder on the margins of Heian civilization.

Tengu in Japanese folklore evolved from fearsome bird-demons who kidnapped people and started wars in the 8th-12th centuries, to mountain-dwelling martial arts masters and protectors of the dharma by the Edo period (1603-1868). The shift reflects Buddhism's absorption of indigenous kami.

The Penanggalan of Malay folklore is a detached female head that flies through the night trailing its entrails, seeking the blood of newborns. Thorny plants (especially jeruju) placed around houses are believed to snag its dangling intestines and trap it until dawn.

Stuart Clark's 'Thinking with Demons' (1997) is the landmark academic work arguing that early modern demonology was not mere superstition but a coherent intellectual system embedded in the period's understanding of science, religion, and politics.

Lilith appears in the Dead Sea Scrolls, the Alphabet of Ben Sira (c. 700-1000 CE), and Babylonian incantation bowls as a night demon and Adam's first wife. Her transformation from Mesopotamian wind spirit to feminist icon spans 4,000 years of continuous reinterpretation.

The Adze of the Ewe people of Ghana and Togo is a vampire-like spirit that takes the form of a firefly. In human form it possesses people and causes disease, particularly in children. It cannot be fought directly and must be driven out through ritual specialists.

Giuseppe Tartini claimed his 'Devil's Trill Sonata' (1713) was inspired by a dream in which the devil sat at the foot of his bed and played a violin piece of such beauty that Tartini wept. The piece he wrote upon waking, he said, was a pale shadow of what the devil played.

The exorcism of Anneliese Michel (1975-1976) in Germany involved 67 exorcism sessions over 10 months. She died of starvation and dehydration at age 23. Her parents and two priests were convicted of negligent homicide. The case inspired the film 'The Exorcism of Emily Rose' (2005).

In Haitian Vodou, the Loa are not demons but spirits that serve as intermediaries between God (Bondye) and humanity. However, the Petwo Loa — fiery, aggressive spirits associated with Kongo traditions — were demonized by colonial missionaries, creating a lasting mischaracterization.

Belphegor, originally the Moabite deity Baal-Peor, became the demon of sloth and invention in Christian demonology. His association with toilets (where he was supposedly worshipped) led to the apocryphal legend that he was Hell's ambassador to France.

The Phi of Thai folklore constitute over 30 distinct spirit types. Phi Pop is a liver-eating possession spirit, Phi Krasue is a floating head with dangling organs (related to the Penanggalan), and Phi Tai Hong is the particularly dangerous ghost of someone who died a violent death.

King James VI of Scotland personally authored 'Daemonologie' (1597), a treatise defending the reality of witchcraft and demonic pacts. Written partly in response to Reginald Scot's skeptical 'Discoverie of Witchcraft,' it shaped witch-hunting policy when James became King of England in 1603.

The Manananggal of Filipino folklore splits its body at the waist at night, growing bat-like wings on its upper half to hunt pregnant women. Sprinkling salt or crushed garlic on the abandoned lower half prevents it from rejoining, killing it at sunrise.

Mahishasura, the buffalo-demon of Hindu mythology, obtained a boon that no man or god could kill him. The goddess Durga was created by the combined energies of all the gods specifically to destroy him — the victory is celebrated during the festival of Navaratri.

Ed and Lorraine Warren investigated over 10,000 cases of alleged demonic activity from the 1950s through 2000s. Their most famous case, the Amityville haunting (1975), remains deeply controversial — skeptics have identified extensive fabrication, while believers cite the Warrens' documentation.

The concept of 'demonic hierarchy' in Western demonology mirrors feudal European social structure. Wierus's Pseudomonarchia Daemonum organizes Hell as a monarchy with kings, dukes, and counts — projecting human political order onto the supernatural.

Gustave Doré's illustrations for Milton's Paradise Lost (1866) created the definitive visual vocabulary for Satan and fallen angels in Western culture. His depiction of Lucifer as a brooding, muscular figure with bat wings influenced every subsequent representation from comics to cinema.

In Norse mythology, the draugr are undead beings that guard their burial mounds with superhuman strength. Unlike passive ghosts, draugr actively terrorize the living, grow in size, and can only be destroyed by a hero who wrestles them back into their graves.

The Testament of Solomon (1st-5th century CE) is the oldest known text of ritual magic, presented as King Solomon's first-person account of binding demons to build the Temple. Each demon reveals its name, power, and the angel that thwarts it — establishing the template for all later grimoires.

The Chinese classification of gui (ghosts/demons) includes Nu Gui (vengeful female ghosts), Shui Gui (water ghosts who drown victims to take their place), E Gui (hungry ghosts), and Jiang Shi (hopping vampires) — each requiring specific ritual countermeasures.

Walter Stephens' 'Demon Lovers' (2002) argues that early modern demonologists were not sadists but anxious theologians who used witch trials to prove the reality of the spiritual world — physical evidence of demonic copulation proved that immaterial beings existed, and therefore so did God.

In Candomblé (Brazilian syncretic religion), Exu is a trickster orisha often misidentified as the devil by Christian missionaries. Exu governs crossroads, communication, and chaos — he is amoral rather than evil, and no ritual can proceed without first honoring him.

The Greek concept of 'daimon' (δαίμων) originally referred to any divine power or spirit — Socrates famously claimed a personal daimonion that guided him. The word only acquired exclusively negative connotations after being repurposed by early Christian writers to describe fallen angels.

Reginald Scot's 'Discoverie of Witchcraft' (1584) systematically debunked demonological beliefs, exposing stage magic tricks behind supposed supernatural events. King James I ordered all copies burned upon his accession to the English throne in 1603.

The Slavic demon Baba Yaga lives in a hut that walks on chicken legs and is surrounded by a fence of human bones topped with skulls. She can be either a fearsome child-eater or a wise helper depending on the tale — reflecting the ambivalence of Slavic spirit traditions.

The Book of Abramelin (c. 1458, published 1725) describes an 18-month purification ritual culminating in contact with one's Holy Guardian Angel, after which the magician gains power over 316 named demons organized under four sub-princes of Hell. Aleister Crowley considered it the most important grimoire.

Kali, the Hindu goddess of time and destruction, is depicted with a garland of demon heads and a skirt of severed arms. Despite her terrifying appearance, she is worshipped as a protective mother figure — her destruction of demons (particularly Raktabija) symbolizes the annihilation of ego.

The Witchcraft Act of 1604 in England, influenced by King James's Daemonologie, made invoking evil spirits a capital offense. It was not repealed until 1736, when it was replaced by a law punishing people who pretended to practice witchcraft — reflecting the Enlightenment shift.

In Egyptian mythology, Apophis (Apep) was a primordial serpent demon of chaos who attacked Ra's solar barque each night during its journey through the underworld. Priests performed daily rituals called 'Banishing Apophis' involving the destruction of wax effigies.

The Basque Akerbeltz (Black Goat) was a pre-Christian spirit of healing and animal protection that was demonized during the witch trials into the goat presiding over sabbaths. This transformation illustrates how indigenous European deities became demons through Christian reinterpretation.

The Salem witch trials of 1692 resulted in 20 executions and involved elaborate demonological testimony. Spectral evidence — claims that a defendant's spirit appeared to the accuser — was eventually ruled inadmissible, ending the trials and establishing a legal precedent still cited today.

Iblis in Islamic theology refused God's command to bow before Adam, arguing that fire (his substance) was superior to clay (Adam's). Unlike the Christian Lucifer narrative, Iblis's rebellion stems from theological pride and rational argumentation, making him a more philosophically complex figure.

The Dybbuk in Jewish folklore is the dislocated soul of a dead person that attaches to a living person's body. Exorcism (performed by a tzaddik or rabbi) involves communicating with the spirit to determine its unfinished business and guiding it to rest through prayer and the blowing of the shofar.

Black metal's relationship with demonology began in earnest with Venom's 1981 album 'Welcome to Hell' and solidified with Bathory, Mayhem, and the Norwegian black metal scene of the early 1990s, which moved from theatrical Satanism to church burnings as praxis.

The Nuckelavee of Orcadian (Scottish) folklore is a skinless horse-demon from the sea whose breath wilts crops and causes plague. It is the most feared creature in the Northern Isles and can only be stopped by fresh running water, which it cannot cross.

Lamashtu, the Mesopotamian demon goddess, was unique in that she acted on her own malevolent will rather than being sent by the gods. She preyed on unborn children and infants. Her image — lion-headed, donkey-toothed, nursing a pig and dog — appears on protective amulets from 1st millennium BCE.

The Sumerian demon Alû was described as a faceless, half-human creature that lurked in dark corners and attacked sleeping people, causing night terrors and sleep paralysis. This 4,000-year-old description closely matches modern clinical descriptions of sleep paralysis hallucinations.

Francesco Guazzo's Compendium Maleficarum (1608) contains woodcut illustrations of eleven stages of the Sabbath, including: renunciation of Christian faith, re-baptism in the devil's name, removal of baptismal chrism, swearing an oath on a 'black book,' and a celebratory feast.

Vetala in Hindu folklore are spirits that inhabit and reanimate corpses, hanging upside-down from trees in cremation grounds. The Baital Pachisi (25 Tales of the Vetala) is a frame narrative where King Vikramaditya must carry a vetala who tells riddles — inspiring the 'Vampire' in Burton's translation.

The Wendigo of Algonquian tradition is a cannibalistic spirit associated with winter famine. Those who resort to cannibalism are believed to become Wendigos — endlessly hungry despite growing larger with each meal. The concept was adapted into the psychiatric diagnosis 'Wendigo psychosis.'

Pierre de Lancre, a French judge, conducted a massive witch hunt in the Basque region of Labourd in 1609, interrogating over 600 people. His 'Tableau de l'Inconstance des Mauvais Anges et Demons' (1612) provides one of the most detailed accounts of alleged sabbath practices.

The Churel of South Asian folklore is the ghost of a woman who died during pregnancy or childbirth, recognized by backward-facing feet. She seduces men and drains their vitality. Iron objects, especially nails driven into the threshold, are used as protection.

Aeshma Daeva, the Zoroastrian demon of wrath and fury, is likely the etymological and mythological source for the Jewish/Christian demon Asmodeus. The transformation from Iranian to Semitic demonology occurred during the Babylonian Exile (586-538 BCE).

The Qareen in Islamic tradition is a personal jinn assigned to every human at birth. While it usually whispers evil suggestions, the Prophet Muhammad reportedly converted his Qareen to Islam, making it advise only good — a concept unique to Islamic demonology.

Cotton Mather's 'Wonders of the Invisible World' (1693) was written to defend the Salem witch trials, providing demonological justification for spectral evidence. His father Increase Mather simultaneously published 'Cases of Conscience,' which argued against spectral evidence — father and son publicly disagreed.

The Picatrix (Ghayat al-Hakim, c. 10th-11th century) is an Arabic grimoire of astrological magic translated into Latin in 1256. It describes summoning planetary spirits through elaborate rituals involving specific incenses, colors, metals, and spoken invocations timed to celestial configurations.

Tsukumogami in Japanese folklore are everyday objects that become animated spirits after reaching 100 years of age. Umbrellas, lanterns, sandals, and tea kettles can all become yokai — reflecting Shinto's belief that all things possess spiritual essence (kami).

The Lemegeton or Lesser Key of Solomon (17th century) contains five books: Ars Goetia (72 demons), Ars Theurgia-Goetia (spirits of compass directions), Ars Paulina (angels of the hours), Ars Almadel (angels of the altitudes), and Ars Notoria (prayers for knowledge).

Empusa in Greek mythology was a shape-shifting demoness sent by Hecate who seduced travelers on lonely roads and devoured them. She typically appeared as a beautiful woman but had one leg of bronze and one of a donkey — a detail preserved in Aristophanes' comedy 'The Frogs' (405 BCE).

The Marid are the most powerful class of jinn in Islamic demonology, associated with the sea and open water. They are described as the most arrogant and rebellious of all jinn types. King Solomon is said to have imprisoned Marid in copper vessels sealed with his ring.

Rakshasas in the Mahabharata include the virtuous Ghatotkacha (son of Bhima and the Rakshasi Hidimbi), who fought heroically for the Pandavas and sacrificed himself to save Arjuna from Karna's divine weapon — demonstrating that Rakshasas could be moral agents.

The Urbain Grandier case (Loudun, 1634) produced documents allegedly signed by demons during exorcisms. One surviving 'pact with the devil' bears signatures attributed to Satanas, Beelzebub, Lucifer, Elimi, Leviathan, and Astaroth — written backward in a mixture of Latin and unknown symbols.

In Celtic mythology, the Fomorians were a race of demonic beings associated with chaos, darkness, and the sea who opposed the Tuatha Dé Danann. Their king Balor had a destructive eye that could kill with its gaze — defeated at the Second Battle of Mag Tuired.

The Strix in Roman belief was a bird-demon that fed on human flesh and blood, particularly targeting infants. Ovid describes strix attacks in his Fasti. The modern Italian word 'strega' (witch) derives from this creature, connecting Roman demonology to medieval witch beliefs.

Nachzehrer in German and Slavic folklore are a type of undead that devour their own burial shrouds and then their own flesh in the grave, mystically causing family members above ground to waste away. Placing a coin in the mouth or a stone under the chin was believed to prevent this.

The Aztec Tzitzimitl were skeletal female deities associated with stars who would descend to devour humanity during solar eclipses. During the New Fire Ceremony held every 52 years, all fires were extinguished — if the ceremony failed, the Tzitzimitl would destroy the world.

Dante's Inferno (1320) organized Hell into nine concentric circles, each guarded by specific demons: Minos judges the damned, Cerberus guards the gluttons, Plutus the hoarders, and Lucifer himself is frozen in the ninth circle — establishing the geography of Hell for Western civilization.

The Gashadokuro of Japanese folklore is a giant skeleton formed from the accumulated bones of people who died of starvation. Standing fifteen times taller than a human, it roams after midnight, capturing lone travelers and biting off their heads. Only a Shinto charm can detect its approach.

Agares, the first Duke of the eastern region of Hell according to the Ars Goetia, appears as an old man riding a crocodile with a goshawk on his fist. He teaches languages, causes earthquakes, and commands 31 legions. The specific absurdity of the imagery is characteristic of Goetic descriptions.

The Berith of demonology is likely derived from the Hebrew word for 'covenant' (brit). In the Ars Goetia, he is a Duke of Hell who rides a red horse, wears a crown, and speaks with a clear voice. He can turn all metals into gold and was historically invoked by alchemists.

The concept of demonic possession varies fundamentally across cultures: Christianity frames it as invasion by evil, Vodou as voluntary mounting by spirits, Hinduism as karmic affliction, and Japanese Shinto as spiritual pollution (kegare) requiring purification rather than exorcism.

Surgat, a minor demon mentioned in the Grimorium Verum (18th century), has the specific power of opening any lock. Unlike the grand princes of Hell, Surgat represents the 'working class' of demonology — spirits invoked for practical, mundane purposes rather than cosmic power.

The Chenoo of Wabanaki (northeastern Native American) tradition is a stone-hearted ice giant created when a human's heart freezes from cruelty or cannibalism. Unlike the Wendigo, a Chenoo can sometimes be cured by melting its frozen heart through acts of kindness and feeding it hot tallow.

Al-Jahiz's 'Kitab al-Hayawan' (Book of Animals, 9th century) contains one of the earliest systematic Islamic taxonomies of jinn, describing their habitats, behaviors, and relationships with humans — treating them as a natural phenomenon rather than purely religious concern.

The Preta (hungry ghosts) in Buddhist cosmology are beings reborn into a realm of insatiable hunger due to greed in past lives. Depicted with enormous stomachs and needle-thin throats, they cannot eat or drink — a visceral metaphor for attachment and unfulfilled desire.

Marchosias in the Ars Goetia is a Marquis of Hell who appears as a she-wolf with griffin wings and a serpent's tail, breathing fire. Before the Fall, she was an angel of the Order of Dominations and told Solomon she hoped to return to heaven after 1,200 years — a rare note of demonic redemption.

The Aswang Inquiry Commission was established by Philippine President Marcos in 1971 to investigate widespread reports of aswang attacks in Capiz province. The commission concluded the attacks were caused by mass hysteria, but the cultural belief remains deeply embedded in Filipino rural life.

Naberius (also Cerberus or Naberus) is a Marquis of Hell in the Ars Goetia who appears as a three-headed dog — clearly derived from the Greek Cerberus. He restores lost honors and teaches rhetoric and the liberal sciences. The Greek-to-demonic transformation exemplifies cultural demon-borrowing.

The Bamberger witch trials (1626-1631) in the Prince-Bishopric of Bamberg resulted in approximately 1,000 executions, including the vice-chancellor and several mayors. Surviving letters from condemned prisoners describe torture techniques designed to extract demonological confessions.

In Hawaiian mythology, the Mo'o are shapeshifting water dragons that can appear as beautiful women. They guard freshwater sources and can be either benevolent or malicious. Specific mo'o like Mo'oinanea are revered as ancestral guardians of particular pools and streams.

Beelzebub's name derives from the Philistine deity Baal-Zebub ('Lord of the Flies') mentioned in 2 Kings 1:2. Some scholars argue the original form was Baal-Zebul ('Lord of the High Place'), deliberately corrupted by Hebrew scribes to insult a rival god.

The Ushi-oni (ox-demon) of Japanese folklore haunts coastal areas, particularly in the Shikoku and Kii regions. It has the body of a spider or crab and the head of an ox, and its appearance presages terrible storms. It often works in tandem with the Nure-onna (wet woman) serpent spirit.

Alfonso de Spina's 'Fortalitium Fidei' (1459) proposed one of the first systematic classifications of demons into ten categories including: those who deceive through dreams, those who cause incubi/succubi attacks, those who disguise themselves as dead relatives, and those who tempt hermits.

The Popobawa of Zanzibar is a one-eyed bat demon that attacks sleepers at night, particularly during election seasons. Outbreaks of Popobawa panic in 1965, 1970, 1995, and 2001 have been documented — anthropologists link its appearances to periods of political tension.

Hecate in Greek religion evolved from a benevolent goddess of crossroads, moonlight, and magic into a queen of ghosts and demons in later Roman and medieval tradition. Her trinity of forms (maiden, mother, crone) was reinterpreted as mastery over earth, sea, and sky.

The Grimorium Verum (1517, likely 18th century) is considered one of the most dangerous grimoires because it provides explicit instructions for making pacts with demons. Unlike the Goetia's binding approach, it teaches negotiation and contract — treating demons as business partners.

Vritra in the Rigveda is a serpent-demon (Ahi) who blockades the cosmic waters, causing drought. Indra's slaying of Vritra with his vajra (thunderbolt) is the central myth of Vedic religion — a primordial act of creation through the defeat of chaos.

The Kumiho of Korean folklore is a nine-tailed fox that must consume 1,000 human livers over 1,000 years to become fully human. Unlike the Japanese kitsune, which can be benevolent, the kumiho is almost always depicted as irredeemably predatory.

Paimon, a King of Hell in the Ars Goetia, appears as a man riding a dromedary camel, preceded by a host of spirits playing trumpets. He teaches all arts, sciences, and secrets, and can reveal what lies in the depths of the sea and the mind. Featured prominently in the 2018 film Hereditary.

The Djinn are mentioned 29 times in the Quran, most significantly in Surah Al-Jinn (72), which describes a group of jinn who heard the Quran and converted to Islam. Islamic theology considers jinn morally autonomous beings capable of both faith and sin.

Stolas (also Stolos) in the Ars Goetia is a Great Prince of Hell who appears as either a crowned owl or a human form. He teaches astronomy, the properties of herbs and precious stones — essentially a demon of natural philosophy and proto-science.

The Chort in Slavic folklore is a devil figure often depicted as a black humanoid with horns, hooves, and a tail. Unlike the terrifying Western Satan, the Chort is frequently foolish and outwitted by clever peasants in folk tales — a domesticated, comedic devil.

Ruqyah is the Islamic practice of spiritual healing through Quranic recitation to treat jinn possession (mass). Specific surahs used include Al-Fatiha, Al-Baqarah (verse 255, Ayat al-Kursi), Al-Falaq, and An-Nas. The practice is distinguished from sorcery, which is haram.

The Trial of the Pendle Witches (1612) in Lancashire, England, resulted in 10 hangings based on testimony involving demonic familiars — animal spirits that served witches. The detailed court records by Thomas Potts provide one of the most complete accounts of English demonological prosecution.

Tiamat in the Enuma Elish (Babylonian creation myth) is a primordial sea goddess who creates eleven monsters — including the mushussu dragon and scorpion-men — to wage war against the younger gods. Marduk's slaying of Tiamat and splitting her body to create heaven and earth parallels other demon-slaying creation myths.

The Yuki-onna (snow woman) of Japanese folklore appears during snowstorms as a tall, beautiful woman with pale or transparent skin. She breathes on travelers to freeze them to death. In some tales she spares men who are handsome or who promise to keep her existence secret.

Alloces in the Ars Goetia is a Duke of Hell who rides an enormous horse with dragon feet and has a leonine face with fiery eyes. He teaches astronomy and liberal sciences, and his voice is described as hoarse and very loud. He commands 36 legions.

The Mara of Scandinavian folklore (distinct from Buddhist Mara) is a female spirit that sits on sleepers' chests causing nightmares — the English word 'nightmare' literally derives from 'night-mare.' Sleep paralysis across cultures is consistently attributed to demonic visitation.

Sitri, a Great Prince of Hell in the Ars Goetia, appears with a leopard's face and griffin wings. His specific power is causing men and women to fall passionately in love — and to make women display themselves naked. He commands 60 legions of spirits.

The Banshee (bean sídhe, 'woman of the fairy mound') of Irish folklore is not a demon but a fairy being whose wailing foretells death. Specific noble families had their own banshees. The Christianization of Irish folklore gradually recast benign fairy beings as demonic.

Narasimha, the man-lion avatar of Vishnu, was specifically created to circumvent the boon granted to the demon Hiranyakashipu, who could not be killed by man or animal, indoors or outdoors, by day or night, on earth or in sky, by any weapon. Narasimha killed him at twilight, on a threshold, on his lap, with his claws.

The Principality of Wallachia under Vlad III (Vlad the Impaler, r. 1448-1476) gave rise to vampire folklore that eventually produced Bram Stoker's Dracula (1897). The historical Vlad's psychological warfare through mass impalement created a lasting association between the region and demonic evil.

Forneus in the Ars Goetia is a Great Marquis of Hell who appears as a sea monster. He teaches rhetoric, gives good names and reputations, and makes enemies into friends. His aquatic form connects to ancient associations between the chaotic sea and demonic power.

The Yaoguai of Chinese mythology are animals, plants, or objects that have absorbed enough qi over centuries to take human form. Unlike inherently evil Western demons, yaoguai can be moral or immoral — the White Snake Lady (Bai Suzhen) is a python-yaoguai and the romantic heroine of one of China's four great folk tales.

The Talmudic tradition holds that demons (shedim) were created at twilight on the sixth day of creation — God made their souls but Shabbat arrived before He could make their bodies, leaving them as disembodied spirits. This explains their invisible, liminal nature.

Aamon (Amon) in the Ars Goetia is a Marquis of Hell who appears as a wolf with a serpent's tail that can assume human form with an owl's head. He reconciles feuds and reveals the past and future. Some scholars connect him to the Egyptian god Amun.

The Mora of South Slavic tradition is a malevolent spirit that enters bedrooms through keyholes and sits on sleepers' chests to feed on their life force. People born with a caul (amniotic membrane) were believed to become mora after death.

The Conjuration of the Four, attributed to Eliphas Lévi (1854), invokes the elemental spirits through the four directions: salamanders of fire, sylphs of air, undines of water, and gnomes of earth. This system bridges Renaissance demonology with 19th-century occultism.

Mammon, though never explicitly named as a demon in the Bible (Matthew 6:24 uses 'mammon' as an Aramaic word for wealth), was personified as a demon of greed by early Church Fathers. Peter Binsfeld's 1589 classification paired him with the deadly sin of avarice.

The Penangga of Malay and Indonesian folklore are similar to the Penanggalan but specifically target fishermen at sea. They manifest as ghostly lights over the water, luring boats into dangerous waters. Offerings of rice and eggs at the bow of fishing boats serve as protection.

Wierus's Pseudomonarchia Daemonum lists Bael as the first king of Hell, commanding 66 legions of demons. He appears with three heads — toad, man, and cat — and grants invisibility. The name derives from the Canaanite deity Ba'al, illustrating how rival gods become demons.

The Leanan Sídhe of Irish and Scottish Gaelic folklore is a fairy lover who inspires artists and poets but drains their life force, causing them to die young. She is considered responsible for the Romantic tradition's association between artistic genius and early death.

Peter Binsfeld's 1589 classification assigned each of the seven deadly sins to a specific demon prince: Lucifer (pride), Mammon (greed), Asmodeus (lust), Leviathan (envy), Beelzebub (gluttony), Satan (wrath), and Belphegor (sloth). This schema dominated Catholic demonology for centuries.

The Barong and Rangda dance drama of Bali depicts the eternal battle between the lion-spirit Barong (good) and the witch-queen Rangda (evil). Performers enter genuine trance states and stab themselves with krises — the Barong's magic prevents injury. UNESCO recognizes it as intangible cultural heritage.

Andras in the Ars Goetia is a Great Marquis of Hell who appears as an angel with an owl's head, riding a black wolf and carrying a sharp sword. He is the most dangerous of the 72 demons — he sows discord and can kill the magician and their assistants if not carefully controlled.

The Tiyanak of Filipino folklore is the spirit of an unbaptized baby that takes the form of a crying infant in the forest. When picked up by a compassionate traveler, it transforms into a fanged monster and attacks. It reflects Catholic influence on indigenous Filipino spirit beliefs.

William Blake's 'The Marriage of Heaven and Hell' (1790-1793) radically reinterpreted demonology by arguing that 'Energy' (traditionally demonic) and 'Reason' (traditionally angelic) are both necessary. His Proverbs of Hell celebrated transgression, influencing Romantic Satanism.

The Tuurngait in Inuit shamanism are spirits that can be either helping spirits or malevolent entities. An angakkuq (shaman) must undergo a dangerous initiation involving spirit dismemberment and reconstitution to gain power over tuurngait — a process that mirrors shamanic ordeals worldwide.

Abezethibou, described in the Testament of Solomon, is a one-winged fallen angel who was trapped inside a pillar of water in the Red Sea. He claims to have been the demon who hardened Pharaoh's heart against Moses and turned the Egyptian magicians' rods into serpents.

The Jikininki of Japanese Buddhism are ghosts of selfish people reborn as corpse-eating demons. They appear human by day but feast on the dead at night, fully aware of their horrific condition but unable to stop — a karmic punishment that embodies Buddhist teachings on attachment.

The Würzburg witch trials (1626-1631) executed an estimated 900 people, including children as young as nine. Bishop Philipp Adolf von Ehrenberg drove the prosecutions using demonological interrogation protocols that extracted confessions about nocturnal sabbaths and demonic pacts.

In Vodou, Baron Samedi is the Loa of death who stands at the crossroads between life and the afterlife. He wears a top hat, tuxedo, and sunglasses and speaks in nasal tones. Despite his frightening role, he is a protector of children and a healer — death as guardian, not destroyer.

Valek (Valak) in the Ars Goetia is a President of Hell who appears as a small boy with angel wings riding a two-headed dragon. He gives true answers about hidden treasures and reveals where serpents can be found. The 2016 film 'The Conjuring 2' reinvented Valak as a demonic nun.

The Kelpie of Scottish folklore is a shape-shifting water horse that lures travelers onto its back, then drags them into lochs to drown and devour them. Its mane is perpetually dripping wet — the tell that distinguishes it from a real horse.

Samael in Jewish Kabbalistic tradition is the angel of death, the accuser (Satan), and the husband of Lilith. Unlike Christian Satan, Samael serves a necessary cosmological function — he is the severity of God's judgment, not a rebel against it.

The Berserkers of Norse tradition were warriors who entered battle in a trance-like fury attributed to possession by animal spirits (bear or wolf). Old Norse sources use language indistinguishable from descriptions of demonic possession, suggesting a culturally sanctioned form of spirit riding.

Xing Tian in Chinese mythology was a giant who challenged the Yellow Emperor and was beheaded. His headless body continued to fight using his nipples as eyes and navel as a mouth — representing the unconquerable will that persists beyond death and dismemberment.

Rosemary's Baby (1968), directed by Roman Polanski and based on Ira Levin's novel, transformed demonology in popular culture by placing demonic conspiracy in a mundane Manhattan apartment building. The film's suggestion that Satan fathered a child updated the medieval incubus tradition for modern audiences.

The Aka Manto (Red Cape) of Japanese urban legend is a spirit that haunts bathroom stalls, asking occupants whether they want red or blue paper. Choosing red results in being flayed alive; choosing blue results in strangulation. The only escape is to refuse both options.

Orobas in the Ars Goetia is a Great Prince of Hell who appears as a horse that transforms to human form at the magician's request. Unusually, he is described as being faithful to the conjurer and not allowing any spirit to tempt him — a uniquely trustworthy demon.

The Incubus and Succubus tradition was systematized by Thomas Aquinas, who argued in the Summa Theologica that demons could collect semen as succubi and implant it as incubi, enabling demonic reproduction without violating the principle that immaterial beings cannot create life.

The Mogwai of Chinese folklore are demonic spirits that harm humans, with power inversely proportional to the victim's moral strength. The name was appropriated for the 1984 film 'Gremlins' — the original mogwai were far more dangerous than Spielberg's version.

Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa's 'De Occulta Philosophia' (1531-1533) synthesized classical demonology, Kabbalah, Neoplatonism, and Hermetic philosophy into a comprehensive magical system. It was simultaneously condemned as heretical and used as a textbook by intellectuals across Europe.

The Loogaroo of Caribbean folklore (from French loup-garou) is a woman who makes a pact with the devil, shedding her skin at night to fly as a ball of fire and suck blood from victims. Placing rice or sand outside a doorway forces her to count every grain before entering.

King Solomon's ring, inscribed with the Seal of Solomon (a hexagram or pentagram), was said to grant him power over all demons. The tradition appears in the Testament of Solomon (1st-5th century CE) and was elaborated in Islamic tradition where Sulayman commanded jinn to build the First Temple.

The Alp of German folklore is a vampire-like elf associated with nightmares. It wears a tarnkappe (hat of concealment) that grants invisibility and enters bedrooms through keyholes. Removing its hat while it sits on a sleeper's chest traps it and forces it to grant wishes.

Dantalion in the Ars Goetia is a Duke of Hell who appears as a man with many faces (both male and female). He knows all human thoughts and can change them at will, and he can show any person's exact double regardless of where they are in the world.

The Obayifo of Ashanti (Ghanaian) folklore is a living witch who leaves their body at night as a ball of light to suck blood from sleeping children and blight crops. Unlike dead spirits, the obayifo is a living person who may not even know they carry the curse.

Michael Psellus's 'De Operatione Daemonum' (11th century) classified demons into six types based on elemental habitation: Leliurium (fiery, in the ether), Aërial (in the atmosphere), Terrestrial (on earth), Aqueous (in water), Subterranean (underground), and Lucifugous (light-fleeing, in caverns).

The Naga in Hindu and Buddhist mythology are serpent beings that dwell in underwater palaces. While sometimes dangerous, they are primarily guardians of treasure and dharma. The seven-headed Naga Mucalinda sheltered the Buddha during meditation — a protective demon, not an adversarial one.

Beleth in the Ars Goetia is a King of Hell who arrives on a pale horse accompanied by trumpets, musicians, and a parade. He is so terrible that the magician must draw a protective triangle and hold a hazel wand toward the southeast. He causes love between men and women.

The Soucouyant of Trinidadian folklore is an old woman who sheds her skin at night and flies as a fireball to suck blood. Her skin must be found and covered with salt to prevent her from re-entering it. She will then be trapped outside her body and die at sunrise.

The Grand Grimoire (Le Dragon Rouge), allegedly dating to 1521 but likely 18th century, contains the most elaborate ritual for summoning Lucifuge Rofocale, Prime Minister of Hell. The ritual requires a bloodstone, two blessed candles, and must be performed during the June solstice.

Rakshasa culture in the Ramayana is sophisticated — Lanka under Ravana had advanced architecture, arts, and governance. This complexity distinguishes Hindu demonology from Western traditions where demons are purely destructive, reflecting the Hindu understanding that asura and deva share divine origins.

The Nuctemeron of Apollonius of Tyana describes 12 hours of the night, each governed by different spirits and demons. The 7th hour is ruled by spirits of anger, the 10th by spirits of divine vengeance — structuring time itself as a demonological phenomenon.

The Wild Hunt is a pan-European folk myth of spectral hunters riding through the night sky, led by figures including Odin, Herne the Hunter, or King Arthur depending on region. Witnesses were believed to be carried away to the underworld or driven mad.

The Benandanti of Friuli, Italy (documented in Inquisition records 1575-1650) were a folk tradition of 'good walkers' who claimed to leave their bodies at night to battle witches and demons for the fertility of crops. The Inquisition gradually forced them to confess to being witches themselves.

Aim (also Aini or Haborym) in the Ars Goetia is a Duke of Hell who appears as a handsome man with three heads — serpent, man with two stars on forehead, and cat. He rides a viper and carries a firebrand with which he sets cities, castles, and great places on fire.

The Jiangshi (Chinese hopping vampire) is a reanimated corpse that kills living creatures to absorb their qi. It moves by hopping because rigor mortis prevents normal walking. Taoist priests use yellow paper talismans inscribed with sacred characters attached to its forehead to immobilize it.

Father Gabriele Amorth served as the Vatican's chief exorcist for 30 years (1986-2016), claiming to have performed over 60,000 exorcisms. His books, including 'An Exorcist Tells His Story' (1999), are the most widely read modern accounts of Catholic demonological practice.

The Acheri of Native American and Indian folklore is the ghost of a young girl who descends from mountain tops to spread disease among villages, especially targeting children. Wearing a red thread around the neck is the traditional protection against her plague.

Demonology experienced a scholarly renaissance in the 1970s-80s through the work of historians like Norman Cohn ('Europe's Inner Demons,' 1975), Carlo Ginzburg ('The Night Battles,' 1966), and Keith Thomas ('Religion and the Decline of Magic,' 1971), who treated demonological beliefs as windows into early modern mentalities.

Glasya-Labolas in the Ars Goetia is a President and Earl of Hell who appears as a dog with griffin wings. He teaches all arts and sciences instantly, causes bloodshed, can make a person invisible, and knows the past and future. He is also described as the author of murder.

The Sack Man (El Coco/Bag Man) appears across Spanish, Portuguese, and Latin American folklore as a shapeless demon that carries away misbehaving children in a sack. The tradition dates to at least medieval Iberia and likely influenced similar bogeymen across colonial territories.

Ronove in the Ars Goetia is a Marquis and Great Earl of Hell who takes the form of a monster. He teaches rhetoric, gives faithful servants and the favor of friends and foes, and commands 19 legions of demons. He is also described as knowledgeable about all languages.

The Alukah (leech) mentioned in Proverbs 30:15 was interpreted by medieval Jewish scholars as a female vampire demon with two daughters who cry 'Give! Give!' — connecting Hebrew demonology to broader vampire traditions and prefiguring the Lilith-as-vampire interpretation.

The Bunyip of Aboriginal Australian mythology is a water-dwelling creature associated with waterholes, swamps, and billabongs. Described variously as having flippers, a horse's tail, or a dog's face, its bellowing cry at night warns people away from dangerous water sources.

Oriax (Orias) in the Ars Goetia is a Great Marquis of Hell who appears as a lion riding a horse, with a serpent's tail. He teaches the virtues of the stars and the mansions of the planets, transforms men, gives dignities and confirmations, and commands 30 legions.

The Ajatar (Devil of the Woods) in Finnish mythology is a female spirit that dwells in the forest and causes disease to anyone who looks at her. She suckles serpents and is the mother of the devil in some Karelian traditions. She represents the danger of the untamed wilderness.

Asag in Sumerian mythology was a monstrous demon so hideous that fish boiled alive in his presence. He mated with mountains to produce stone warriors. His defeat by the hero-god Ninurta resulted in the creation of the Tigris and Euphrates rivers from Asag's blood.

The concept of spiritual warfare in Pentecostal Christianity treats demonology as an active, ongoing reality rather than historical artifact. Territorial spirits are believed to control geographic regions, and 'prayer walking' through neighborhoods is practiced to displace demonic influence.

The Toyol of Malay folklore is a child spirit enslaved by a bomoh (shaman) to steal money and valuables. It is kept in a jar and fed with drops of the owner's blood or milk. When the owner dies, the toyol must be passed on or it becomes malevolent.

Caim (Camio) in the Ars Goetia is a Great President of Hell who first appears as a thrush, then transforms into a man carrying a sharp sword. He gives true answers about the future, teaches the language of birds and animals, and once was of the Order of Angels.

The Druj in Zoroastrian theology is the cosmic principle of falsehood and disorder, opposed to Asha (truth). Individual drujes are female demons of specific evils: Druj Nasu (corpse-pollution), Az (greed), Jahi (lewdness), and Bushyasta (sloth that delays morning prayers).

The Kapre of Filipino folklore is a giant tree-dwelling spirit that smokes enormous cigars, making its location detectable by the tobacco smell. While intimidating, it is usually benign unless its tree is harmed. It enjoys disorienting travelers by making them walk in circles.

William Peter Blatty based 'The Exorcist' (1971) on the 1949 exorcism of Roland Doe (pseudonym) in St. Louis, Missouri. The actual case involved a 14-year-old boy, Jesuit priests, and claims of levitation and speaking in Latin — details disputed by subsequent investigators.

Halphas (Malthus) in the Ars Goetia is an Earl of Hell who appears as a stork and speaks with a hoarse voice. He builds towers and furnishes them with ammunition and weapons, sends warriors to appointed places, and commands 26 legions of spirits — a demon of military logistics.

The concept of demonic familiar spirits in English witchcraft trials (1560-1680) was uniquely British. Unlike Continental demonology's focus on sabbaths and pacts, English witch trials centered on relationships with animal spirits — cats, dogs, toads, flies — that served as agents of malice.

In Mesopotamian exorcism texts (Maqlû and Šurpu series, c. 8th-7th century BCE), demons were banished through elaborate incantations combined with the burning of figurines. The priest (āšipu) served as intermediary between gods and demons — the oldest documented exorcism tradition.

Vine in the Ars Goetia is a King and Earl of Hell who appears as a lion riding a black horse and carrying a viper. He discovers hidden things, witches, wizards, and past and future events. He can build towers, demolish stone walls, and make rough waters calm.

The Jorogumo of Japanese folklore is a spider that, upon reaching 400 years of age, gains the ability to shapeshift into a beautiful woman. She lures men into her web, binds them in spider silk, and devours them. The name literally means 'binding bride.'

Nicholas Rémy, judge in Lorraine, France, claimed to have sentenced 900 witches to death over 15 years. His 'Demonolatry' (1595), based on these trial records, describes demons as cold to the touch, incapable of genuine emotion, and possessing bodies that cast no shadow.

The Encantado of Brazilian Amazonian folklore are enchanted beings — often pink river dolphins (boto) — that transform into handsome men or beautiful women at night to seduce humans at riverside festivals. Children born from these unions are said to have supernatural abilities.

Seere (Sear) in the Ars Goetia is a Prince of Hell under Amaymon's rule who appears as a beautiful man riding a winged horse. He can travel the entire world in the twinkling of an eye, bring abundance, and help find hidden treasures. He is described as indifferent to good or evil.

The Wechuge of the Athabaskan Dené people is a person transformed into a cannibalistic monster by breaking a cultural taboo or through a supernatural curse. Unlike the Wendigo, the Wechuge retains human intelligence and often appears as a loved one before attacking.

Scot's 'Discoverie of Witchcraft' (1584) includes the first English-language publication of the complete list of 72 Goetic demons, their seals, and conjuration methods — paradoxically ensuring the survival of the very demonological practices he sought to debunk as fraud.

The Masan of Indian folklore is the spirit of a dead child that haunts cremation grounds and possesses children who wander near burning ghats after dark. It is said to ride on the shoulders of its victim, growing heavier until the child collapses.

Leraje (Leraie) in the Ars Goetia is a Marquis of Hell who appears as a green-clad archer. He causes great battles and disputes, and makes wounds putrefy and gangrenous. He commands 30 legions — a demon specifically associated with the horrors of war wounds.

The Mora Romagnola of Italian folklore is a night demon that paralyzes sleepers by sitting on their chest and squeezing their throat. The victim must wiggle their toes to break free. The mora cannot enter a room where shoes are placed with one pointing toward the bed and one away.

Haagenti in the Ars Goetia is a President of Hell who appears as a bull with griffin wings. He makes men wise by transmuting metals into gold, and turns water into wine and back again. He commands 33 legions — an alchemist's demon with eucharistic overtones.

The Teke Teke of Japanese urban legend is the ghost of a girl who was cut in half by a train. She drags herself on her elbows and hands, making a 'teke-teke' scraping sound, and chases victims to cut them in half with a scythe, creating more Teke Teke.

Ludovico Maria Sinistrari's 'De Daemonialitate' (c. 1680, published 1875) argued that incubi and succubi were not demons but a separate species of rational beings with corporeal bodies, souls, and the capacity for redemption — a remarkably progressive demonological position for its era.

The Sigbin of Visayan Filipino folklore walks backward with its head lowered between its hind legs. It consumes children's shadows, which it stores in a jar. Wealthy families supposedly keep sigbin in jars as amulets that bring prosperity through the collected shadows.

Bifrons in the Ars Goetia is an Earl of Hell who first appears as a monster but then takes human shape at the conjurer's request. He teaches astrology, geometry, and the properties of herbs and stones. His most notable power is moving corpses between graves.

The Pishacha in Hindu demonology are flesh-eating demons that inhabit cremation grounds. They can possess humans, causing insanity (pishacha-grastha). Treatment involves rituals from the Atharva Veda including the Pishacha-moksha ceremony to release the spirit and restore the victim's mind.

The Myling of Scandinavian folklore are the spirits of unbaptized children forced to wander the earth. They chase lonely travelers and leap onto their backs, demanding to be carried to a graveyard for proper burial. As the bearer approaches the cemetery, the myling grows impossibly heavy.

Murmur (Murmus) in the Ars Goetia is a Duke and Earl of Hell who appears as a soldier riding a vulture or griffin, wearing a ducal crown. Two ministers attend him sounding trumpets. He teaches philosophy, constrains the souls of the dead to appear and answer questions.

The Noppera-bō of Japanese folklore is a faceless ghost that appears as a normal person until it turns around to reveal a blank, featureless face. It does not harm victims but the shock of seeing it can cause madness or death from fright.

Thomas Hobbes in 'Leviathan' (1651) rejected the reality of demons entirely, arguing that biblical references to demonic possession were metaphors for disease and madness. His materialist philosophy challenged demonology's intellectual foundations more effectively than any theological argument.
